title: Sarcopharyngia angolensis
taxonomy: Sarcopharyngia angolensis is a species of gastrotrich, which is a type of small, legless, worm-like invertebrate found in freshwater environments. It belongs to the phylum Gastrotricha, which is a group of organisms that are characterized by their unique body structure and the presence of a distinct head and setae. Gastrotricha is a class of pseudocoelomate animals, which means that they have a pseudocoelom, a cavity that is located between the body wall and the digestive tract. Sarcopharyngia angolensis is classified as a member of the family Chaetonotidae, which is a family of gastrotrichs found in freshwater environments around the world. This classification is based on its morphology and other biological characteristics that are unique to this group of organisms.

intro: Sarcopharyngia angolensis, also known as the Angola ghost pipe, is a species of flowering plant in the family Aristolochiaceae, characterized by its unique, hollow, and pipe-like inflorescences. Traditionally, the plant has been used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Bioactive constituents of Sarcopharyngia angolensis include alkaloids, glycosides, and phenolic compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used to make tinctures and extracts, which are used to treat a range of health conditions.
